title: Semiclassical foundation of universality in chaotic quantum circuits
authors: Maximilian F. I. Kieler, Felix Fritzsch, Arnd Bäcker
abstract: The fundamental correspondence between quantum chaotic single-particle systems and random matrix theory is well-understood via periodic orbit theory. In contrast, we show that many-body systems with explicit subsystem structure possess characteristics different from the single-particle theory. We present a periodic orbit theory for many-body systems with well defined semiclassical limit. For this we identify periodic orbit families arising exclusively in the many-body setting and implement a central limit theorem characterizing their correlations. Based on this we demonstrate that spectral correlations in chaotic quantum circuits are characterized by the breaking of individual time translation invariance of periodic orbits in the subsystems into residual synchronous time translations only. This provides a systematic approach to confirming random matrix universality in deterministic many-body systems.
summary: This paper provides a semiclassical framework to explain how Random Matrix Theory universality emerges in many-body quantum circuits. By using periodic orbit theory, the authors show that interactions break individual subsystem symmetries to create a global synchronized state. They further prove that the characteristic RMT spectral signatures are robust even when classical correlations decay slowly.
main_problem: Establishing a semiclassical foundation for the emergence of Random Matrix Theory (RMT) universality in many-body systems with explicit subsystem structures.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that RMT universality arises from the collective synchronization of periodic orbit correlations, where interaction breaks subsystem time-translation symmetry into global synchronous time translations.
method: Periodic orbit theory using Gutzwiller's trace formula, stationary phase approximation, and a mapping of the Spectral Form Factor to a statistical mechanics Potts model.
model_or_system: L-partite chaotic quantum maps representing many-body systems with interacting subsystems, where the semiclassical limit is defined by h-bar approaching zero.
important_parameters: Heisenberg time (T_H), subsystem Heisenberg time, interaction strength (epsilon), scaling parameter (Lambda), and the semiclassical parameter (h-bar).
main_assumptions: Hyperbolicity of the classical maps, instantaneous or subexponential decay of classical correlations, and the existence of a well-defined semiclassical limit.
figures_summary: Figure 1 displays the 'bump-ramp' structure of the Spectral Form Factor in a double-logarithmic scale, comparing calculated values against RMT limiting cases.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of many-body universality, develops a periodic orbit theory for L-partite systems, maps the spectral correlations to a Potts model via a Central Limit Theorem, and proves the robustness of the RMT ramp under subexponential correlation decay.
why_interesting: It provides a fundamental semiclassical derivation for how local subsystem dynamics synchronize to produce global RMT behavior, which is central to understanding many-body chaos and thermalization.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Bump-ramp structure of the SFF, see (11) for χ = 0.975, L = 3 (blue, full) in double-logarithmic scale. The black dashed lines indicate the limiting cases K(T) = T L (for χ = 1) and K(T) = T (for χ = 0). Inset: Schematic struc- ture of a family Γ0 = (γ(1), γ(2)) (magenta dot) for L = 2 and T = 4. The magenta and cyan curves indicate two peri- odic orbits living on Γ0, generated by ϕ(t,t) 0 Γ0 and ϕ(t,t+2) 0 Γ0, respectively. The synchronous time translation ϕ(1,1) 0 is in- dicated by a magenta dashed line and an asynchronous time translation ϕ(0,2) is indicated by a cyan dashed line.
